Scattered severe thunderstorms developed over eastern Ohio as a weak front pushed northeast across the Ohio Valley.

Deep layer southerly flow advected a moist, tropical airmass into the Mid-Atlantic.  High rainfall rates from training thunderstorms in an urban area create an isolated flash flood evening in Berkeley County.  Rainfall amounts of up to 1.5 inches were observed in less than an hour in Martinsburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roadways were reported to have water of depth greater than 6 inches flowing across them.  A nearby spotter measured 1.32 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system, and it's associated cold front crossed through the region late in the day on the 26th. Scattered severe thunderstorms, most of which produced wind damage, developed along the frontal boundary in an area of instability and high shear. A microburst occurred in Monongalia county in West Virginia with an estimated wind speed of 70-80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure over the Midwest remained cutoff from jet stream on the 16th. The warm front associated with this system passed through during the morning. Warm and moist southerly winds behind the front provided moderate amounts of instability. The instability combined with forcing from the low for showers and thunderstorms to develop.||There was enough instability along with strong winds aloft for some thunderstorms to become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust was estimated to be around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms training along a stalled frontal boundary produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding in Cameron, West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flash flooding that affected 48 homes, a school and a church with structural damage due to back water on Cameron Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system and its associated warm front were slowly progressing through the Ohio valley bringing numerous rounds of showers and thunderstorms. Outflow boundaries left over from thunderstorms late on May 12th became a mechanism for re-development ahead and along the warm front on the 13th. Many of these storms were slow moving causing the flooding of many roadways in southwestern Pennsylvania and eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reports that a bridge on Weidman Run Road is flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure rode along a stationary boundary over northern West Virginia, producing scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms. A highly moist atmosphere supported heavy rainfall rates and training activity combined with saturated ground was enough to cause isolated flooding. Rainfall amounts of up to 2.01 were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patterson Creek Road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure both at the surface and the upper levels of the atmosphere passed through the region on the 12th. Showers and thunderstorms developed across the area. Despite relatively dry antecedent conditions, an unusually moist atmosphere combined with significant lift produce high rainfall rates sufficient for flash flooding across the Potomac Highlands. Rainfall amounts of over 3 inches in a several hour period were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of water was flowing across Oakwood Estates Road near Route 220.  A cooperative observer near the event observed 1.16 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front slowly passed through the region during the late evening hours of the 27th into the morning of the 28th. A southerly flow ahead of the front caused deep moisture over the area. The deep moisture combined with a mid-level trough that was associated with the cold front to produce moderate amounts of instability. The cold front combined with the instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Gusty winds aloft were able to mix down to the surface in some of the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down along Route 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge was centered over the southern Plains while an upper-level low was located over central Canada. A northwest flow between these two systems caused colder air aloft to move overhead. Warm and moist air at the surface combined with the colder air aloft to produce moderate amounts of instability. A pressure trough at the surface caused showers and thunderstorms to develop. Moderate instability caused some thunderstorms to produce damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to half dollars in size was reported just off of Route 50.

A pressure trough at the surface caused showers and thunderstorms to develop. Moderate instability caused some thunderstorms to produce damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree branches were blown down along US 50.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level high pressure provided enough subsidence to cause hot conditions on the 21st. Surface high pressure over the Atlantic caused moist air to move into the region from the south. The combination of the heat and humidity caused heat indices over 105 degrees in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices up to 112 degrees were measured at the Cumberland Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through the region on the 28th.  Multiple clusters of storms accompanied the front as it progressed. One of these storms increased to severe levels over southeast West Virginia and produced large hail. Another storm produced enough rain in a short period of time to cause flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as five inches of rain fell in only a 90 minute time period in and around the community of Zenith.  Several roads, including Zenith Road, Wallace Hollow Road and County Road 29/2 - Back County Road, were inundated due to flash flooding of small streams.  The flash flood waters also damaged some downstream outbuildings and vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front slowly passed through the region during the late evening hours of the 27th into the morning of the 28th. A southerly flow ahead of the front caused deep moisture over the area. The deep moisture combined with a mid-level trough that was associated with the cold front to produce moderate amounts of instability. The cold front combined with the instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Gusty winds aloft were able to mix down to the surface in some of the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were down near Wardensville.

A cold front combined with moderate instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms on the twelfth. Stronger winds aloft due to an upper-level low over New England caused thunderstorms to become well organized. Thunderstorms were able to produce damaging wind gusts along wit large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down in the Bakerton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure both at the surface and the upper levels of the atmosphere passed through the region on the 12th. Showers and thunderstorms developed across the area. Despite relatively dry antecedent conditions, an unusually moist atmosphere combined with significant lift produce high rainfall rates sufficient for flash flooding across the Potomac Highlands. Rainfall amounts of over 3 inches in a several hour period were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lunice Creek was overflowing, forcing the closure of Route 42 between Patterson Creek Road and Hott Hill Road.

Slow storm movement and very high rainfall rates prompted an isolated flash flood near Capon Bridge.  A spotter measured 2.80 inches of rain near the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 50 was closed due to flash flooding west of Capon Bridge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree along with large branches were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An east to west oriented cold front sagged south into eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia during the early evening on the 9th.  Temperatures reached the upper 80s with dew points near 70.  There was very little convection over northern West Virginia at 1900E.  Near sunset, with an approaching disturbance in the winds aloft, convection fired in north central West Virginia, with new cells developing to the west during the evening.  Large hail and severe wind gusts occurred in a few locations.||Repetitive showers and the intensity of the rain did cause some local heavier rain amounts.  Small stream and urban flooding occurred in Harrison and Gilmer Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.  A spotter measured a wind gust to 67 mph.

Rain of 1.5 to 2 inches in less than 2 hours caused minor flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over New England pumped in an abundant amount of cold air while low pressure developed along the Mid-Atlantic Coast. Precipitation well ahead of the low overspread eastern West Virginia during the evening hours of the 28th. Precipitation continued into the 29th before ending during the afternoon hours. There was enough cold air for precipitation to fall in the form of snow especially across the Potomac Highlands. ||The combination of heavy wet snow on trees that still had leaves caused numerous power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 6.0 inches at Ranson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over New England pumped in an abundant amount of cold air while low pressure developed along the Mid-Atlantic Coast. Precipitation well ahead of the low overspread eastern West Virginia during the evening hours of the 28th. Precipitation continued into the 29th before ending during the afternoon hours. There was enough cold air for precipitation to fall in the form of snow especially across the Potomac Highlands. ||The combination of heavy wet snow on trees that still had leaves caused numerous power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 8.0 inches about four miles west of Franklin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large disturbance aloft was located over West Virginia. Instability increased during the early afternoon.  Low level convergence along a boundary helped trigger convection.   Due to the low freezing level, hail occurred in a few of the storms.

An east to west oriented cold front sagged south into eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia during the early evening on the 9th.  Temperatures reached the upper 80s with dew points near 70.  There was very little convection over northern West Virginia at 1900E.  Near sunset, with an approaching disturbance in the winds aloft, convection fired in north central West Virginia, with new cells developing to the west during the evening.  Large hail and severe wind gusts occurred in a few locations.||Repetitive showers and the intensity of the rain did cause some local heavier rain amounts.  Small stream and urban flooding occurred in Harrison and Gilmer Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front drifted east into the Potomac Highlands during the afternoon. Thunderstorms trained over the same area for several hours across locations that had saturated ground. Flash flooding resulted in Mineral County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home was flooded in a residental area of Short Gap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front drifted east into the Potomac Highlands during the afternoon. Thunderstorms trained over the same area for several hours across locations that had saturated ground. Flash flooding resulted in Mineral County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mudslides and rockslides were reported along Route 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure moved off the east coast on the 13th. A return flow ushered in a warm and moist airmass across the Potomac Highlands of West Virginia. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed due to the airmass. An isolated thunderstorm did produce large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was estimated near Ruddle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow between upper-level low pressure over the Midwest and upper-level high pressure over the Atlantic Ocean produced a warm and moist airmass over the region. A surface trough also remained overhead during this time. The instability from the airmass along with the surface trough triggered showers and thunderstorms. High amounts of instability along with strong winds aloft caused thunderstorms to become severe with large hail being the primary threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported at Short Gap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between high pressure over the Tennessee Valley and a cold front passing through the Allegheny and Potomac Highlands caused high winds on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 65 mph were reported near Brushy Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary area of upper level low pressure system over the Ohio Valley provided plenty of lift to combine with an usually strong and moist southeasterly low-level flow to produce bands of moderate to heavy showers across the mid-Atlantic.  With saturated ground in place and the training nature of the widespread showers, flood and flash flooding was the result across portions of eastern West Virginia. Rainfall amounts of over 3 inches were common and included a measurement of 3.80 inches from near Kline Gap, WV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tuscarora Creek overflowed its banks forcing the closures of numerous roads.  A spotter near the event reported 3.25 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon as a cold front began to sag across the region on the 3rd. The weak front then stalled along the Mason-Dixon line. As an upper level short wave progressed across the front on July 4th, additional thunderstorms developed. Wind damage and flooding occurred in Pennsylvania, West Virginia and Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reports large trees blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving east across the upper Ohio Valley produce a cluster of thunderstorms with very heavy rain. Rainfall of over 2 inches caused flash flooding across portions of northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement and the public reported flash flooding in and southeast of Fairmont with high water flowing across numerous roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving east across the upper Ohio Valley produce a cluster of thunderstorms with very heavy rain. Rainfall of over 2 inches caused flash flooding across portions of northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flash flooding on sections of SR 26 from Tunnelton to Fellowsville, and flash flooding of sections of U.S. 50 from Fellowsville to Aurora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving east across the upper Ohio Valley produce a cluster of thunderstorms with very heavy rain. Rainfall of over 2 inches caused flash flooding across portions of northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flash flooding with high water flowing across roads from near Blacksville to Morgantown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low center tracked from northern Georgia into the Ohio Valley and brought very strong south to southeast winds into southern West Virginia. A few trees were blown down in the winds which were estimated to have gusted over 58 mph on the higher ridges, although at Bluefield Airport ASOS (KBLF) the maximum gust was 41 mph between 9 and 10 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down along Bent Mountain Road in Lerona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain fell from the 6th into the morning of the 7th.  After 1 to 2 inches of rain, the precipitation changed to wet snow during the early afternoon hours of the 7th across the mountainous counties.  A 2 to 5 inch snow accumulation was common in about 6 hours.  Amounts were highly dependent on elevation.  A maximum snow accumulation of 5 to 7 inches occurred near Pickens, Glady, and Harman of Randolph County.  The snow quickly diminished after sunset, as the strong mid and upper level dynamics raced northeast.   ||Due to the wet nature of the snow, a few power outages did occur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked up the Mid-Atlantic Coast spreading rain across most of West Virginia early on the 6th. As the low passed by to the east, colder air was drawn into the system causing rain to mix with and eventually change to snow before ending early on the 7th. The heaviest snow amounts were across the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were estimated to be around 3 inches across western Mineral County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through the region on the evening of the 21st. Precipitation initially started out as rain before changing over to ice and snow as colder air worked its way in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet totaled up to 2.0 inches at Maysville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of snow showers were associated with the arrival of colder air from late Thursday evening the 6th into Saturday evening the 8th.  The snow showers were the heaviest overnight Friday night, which was late on the 7th into the early hours of the 8th.  The old December snow pack had mostly melted by New Years Day.  This 48 hour episode brought new snow accumulations of 4 to 8 inches.

A strong cold front moved through eastern West Virginia during the morning hours of the 7th. An upper-level low moved into the region from the Tennessee Valley causing surface low pressure to develop along the front. Low pressure allowed for precipitation to continue on the cold side of the front during the afternoon hours of the 7th lasting into the early morning hours of the 8th. There was enough cold air to cause precipitation to end as snow during this time. ||The highest snowfall amounts were along the Allegheny front of eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was estimated to be around six inches across western Pendleton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line/mesoscale convective system that developed out ahead of a surface cold front crossed into lower Michigan early on the 11th. This bowing complex continued to race southeastward producing widespead reports of damage in it's wake. The complex crossed into Ohio by the early afternoon and began to weaken on the northern edge as it reached the Ohio/PA border. Numerous trees were reported down along the center and southern end of the line in eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reports of trees and power lines down in New Martinsville and Pine Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the Midwest on the 9th. Plenty of moisture from the Atlantic Ocean was drawn into this system causing widespread precipitation eastern West Virginia. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of rain, but there was enough cold air for a period of snow near the Potomac Highlands during the morning and afternoon hours of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 5.0 inches near Cherry Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow between upper-level low pressure over the Midwest and upper-level high pressure over the Atlantic Ocean produced a warm and moist airmass over the region. A surface trough also remained overhead during this time. The instability from the airmass along with the surface trough triggered showers and thunderstorms. High amounts of instability along with strong winds aloft caused thunderstorms to become severe with large hail being the primary threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large limb was down along Tablers Station Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through the region during the morning hours. The strong pressure gradient between these two systems caused high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave traveling north over a broad ridge in the central U.S dragged a weak cold front over the Ohio Valley. This coldfront set off thunderstorms in the afternoon and evening hours on the 18th. There were several reports of wind damage in eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania with more than 11,000 people without power in western PA alone. In addition urban flooding and flash flooding occurred in various parts of Allegheny and Beaver counties. Cars were stranded in high water right around rush-hour on Washington Blvd in downtown Pittsburgh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reports a tree down on Sandbag Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed through the Great Lakes on the 10th. A strong cold front associated with this system slowly moved through eastern West Virginia during the afternoon and evening hours. A southeast wind between this system and high pressure over the Atlantic Ocean pumped in copious amounts of moisture across the region. The deep moisture combined with strong lift from the front and limited instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms produced locally heavy amounts of rain in a short period of time which led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported on Caldwell Hollow Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of snow showers were associated with the arrival of colder air from late Thursday evening the 6th into Saturday evening the 8th.  The snow showers were the heaviest overnight Friday night, which was late on the 7th into the early hours of the 8th.  The old December snow pack had mostly melted by New Years Day.  This 48 hour episode brought new snow accumulations of 4 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind flow existed ahead of a cold front.  Thunderstorms were racing northeast at speeds over 60 mph.||The strongest storms moved from Wayne and Lincoln Counties on through Kanawha County to Braxton County.  Wind damage was most widespread in the urban areas around Charleston.  A wind gust to 60 mph was measured at the National Weather Service Office in the Southridge section of Charleston.  A gust of around 70 mph was estimated from Flatwoods of Braxton County.  Numerous trees or large branches were blown down, some onto power lines and houses.  In Kanawha County, 29,000 customers were without power.  Yeager Airport generator failed, causing flights to be delayed and diverted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 60 mph was measured at the National Weather Service Office.  Trees and large limbs were blown down in the vicinity.

Wind damage was most widespread in the urban areas around Charleston.  A wind gust to 60 mph was measured at the National Weather Service Office in the Southridge section of Charleston.  A gust of around 70 mph was estimated from Flatwoods of Braxton County.  Numerous trees or large branches were blown down, some onto power lines and houses.  In Kanawha County, 29,000 customers were without power.  Yeager Airport generator failed, causing flights to be delayed and diverted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to around 70 mph was estimated.

Upper level low pressure remained over the Ohio Valley while high pressure was located over the Atlantic Ocean. A southerly flow between these two systems pumped in plenty of moisture across the area. The deep moisture provided moderate amounts of instability at the same time a cold front was moving in from the west. These two features combined to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of ping pong balls was measured near Shanks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rich tropical moisture streamed northward from the Gulf of Mexico in advance of a slow-moving cold front across the Ohio Valley.  Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed in areas of terrain-enhanced lift across the Potomac Highlands.  A couple of reports of flooding and flash flooding were received after the heavy rainfall amounts from this activity.  The highest rainfall total in the region was observed in Grant County, where 2.05 inches was recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water was hindering traffic flow near the intersection of Routes 42 and 93.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving along a stalled front turned mixed precipitation to heavy wet snow the afternoon of the 21st across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and the northern panhandle of West Virginia. The snow continued into the early morning of the 22nd. Snowfall rates up to 2 inches an hour produced storm totals of 6 to 10 inches across the region. Much of the snow fell during the evening rush hour from Steubenville, Ohio east to Pittsburgh and Greensburg, Pennsylvania bringing the evening commute to a standstill. Numerous cars were abandoned on roadways with some travelers spending nearly 8 hours to reach their destinations. Pockets of freezing rain were south of the area of heavy snow with more than one quarter of an inch of ice reported in Preston county West Virginia.

A deepening low pressure system, originating from the southern plains, moved towards the western Great Lakes on Wednesday March 9th. A wintry mix of precipitation changed over to all rain as the warm front associated with this system pushed across the region on Wednesday morning. Periods of moderate to heavy rain continued through the passage of the upper level trough late Thursday. With much of the region already saturated from previous rainfall over the past 2 weeks many streams and creeks were already elevated. The additional 1 to 2 inches of rainfall resulted in my streams and creeks gong out of their banks throughout east central Ohio, western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reports that Turkeyfoot Rd is flooded along Kings Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold air over the relatively moist Great Lakes triggered snow showers for locations along and west of the Allegheny front during the evening hours of the 6th through the morning hours of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 3.5 inches near Hartmansville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified was located off the New England Coast while high pressure approached from the Ohio Valley. The pressure gradient between these two systems caused breezy conditions. Cold temperatures combined with breezy conditions for extremely low wind chill values near the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values were estimated to be around -20F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold air over the relatively moist Great Lakes triggered snow showers for locations along and west of the Allegheny front during the evening hours of the 6th through the morning hours of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 4.0 inches near Cherry Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving along a stalled front turned mixed precipitation to heavy wet snow the afternoon of the 21st across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and the northern panhandle of West Virginia. The snow continued into the early morning of the 22nd. Snowfall rates up to 2 inches an hour produced storm totals of 6 to 10 inches across the region. Much of the snow fell during the evening rush hour from Steubenville, Ohio east to Pittsburgh and Greensburg, Pennsylvania bringing the evening commute to a standstill. Numerous cars were abandoned on roadways with some travelers spending nearly 8 hours to reach their destinations. Pockets of freezing rain were south of the area of heavy snow with more than one quarter of an inch of ice reported in Preston county West Virginia.

A strong wind flow existed ahead of a cold front.  Thunderstorms were racing northeast at speeds over 60 mph.||The strongest storms moved from Wayne and Lincoln Counties on through Kanawha County to Braxton County.  Wind damage was most widespread in the urban areas around Charleston.  A wind gust to 60 mph was measured at the National Weather Service Office in the Southridge section of Charleston.  A gust of around 70 mph was estimated from Flatwoods of Braxton County.  Numerous trees or large branches were blown down, some onto power lines and houses.  In Kanawha County, 29,000 customers were without power.  Yeager Airport generator failed, causing flights to be delayed and diverted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were blown down.  Siding was ripped off outbuildings.  A trampoline was picked up and thrown about 100 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Monday, March 21st warm frontal showers and thunderstorms moved through during the early morning hours. A cold front moved across the region later in the afternoon bringing another round of showers and isolated thunderstorms. During the afternoon, the better threat for severe storms was located south of I-70 with the best instability over central West Virginia and southeastern Ohio. With instability limited, and a capping inversion in place, most storms did not reach severe criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain began overnight of the 11th into the 12th, around the north side of a strong mid level disturbance.  The rain increased during the daylight hours on the 12th on the southeast flow into Pocahontas County.  Those showers then moved north and northwest through Randolph County.  Rain amounts of less than a half inch were measured by dawn on the 12th.  Another 1 to 1.5 inches of rain fell during the daylight hours on the 12th, before tapering off.  Most maximum rain totals were on either side of 1.5 inches.  ||Minor stream flooding occurred.  A strong rise occurred on headwater rivers, but no significant river flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small stream overflowed across roads.  Basement flooding occurred in Valley Bend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An east to west front sliced through central West Virginia and southern Ohio on the 8th.  A band of rain was seen during the morning along and north of this boundary.  New convection formed during the early afternoon south of the front.  The steering flow was from the northwest at about 35 to 40 mph.  Despite the fast flow, hail was the main element, rather than damaging wind gusts.     ||Behind the first line of convection, a second batch formed in eastern Kentucky and southern West Virginia.  This caused some local training across Lincoln County into Logan and Wyoming Counties into the evening hours.  The town of Logan measured over 2 inches for one of the heaviest amounts.  This rain helped saturate the ground for another rain event, less than 24 hours later, on the 9th.

The rain was its heaviest during the mid morning into the early afternoon.  The rain diminished by mid and late afternoon, as the front lifted north.  By mid afternoon, rain amounts of 1.5 to 2.0 inches were common, generally near the Route 50 corridor.  The ground was already wet prior to this event.  As a result, small stream flooding was common from Barbour County on west through Harrison, Doddridge, and Ritchie Counties. However, these showers and thunderstorms were weakening as they reached into West Virginia.

Upper level low pressure remained over the Ohio Valley while high pressure was located over the Atlantic Ocean. A southerly flow between these two systems pumped in plenty of moisture across the area. The deep moisture provided moderate amounts of instability at the same time a cold front was moving in from the west. These two features combined to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down along Ridge Loop Road.

The elevation above sea level was around 2300 to 2400 feet.  Trees were blown down as well as snapped off above the ground.  Maximum wind gusts near 110 mph were estimated by the survey team. Showers and thunderstorms moved north during the overnight hours, ahead of an approaching cold front.  Just prior to the frontal passage, training of showers affected southern Wayne County on north through parts of Lincoln, Putnam and Jackson Counties from 0600E to 0900E on Saturday the 16th.   Rain amounts of 1 to 1.5 inches occurred there in 3 to 4 hours.  This enhanced the rain totals to around 2 inches in that narrow band. ||Minor flash flooding occurred under the repetitive showers during the morning.  Though, one elderly man drove into high water in Jackson County and drowned.  No homes were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of Twelvepole Creek and its smaller feeder streams flooded and closed roads around Dunlow.   Other small streams in southern Wayne County also flooded roads.

Low pressure moved out of Illinois in the morning, reaching western Pennsylvania by evening.||Individual thunderstorm cells developed in western Ohio and southern Indiana around midday, reaching into West Virginia during the late afternoon and evening.  Hail was initially the main impact, but stronger wind gusts developed as the thunderstorms|matured and consolidated.| |Out ahead, a separate area of showers and thunderstorms bushed southern West Virginia during the late afternoon.

Another major rain event occurred from late on the 9th into the evening hours of the 10th.  Rain amounts of 1 to 2 inches were common. Including this episode, the accumulative affects of 5 significant rain events over the upper Ohio River Valley since February 21st, caused the Ohio River to flood.  The crest along the Ohio River was mostly 2 to 6 feet above the assigned flood stages.  The crest occurred late on the 12th into the morning hours of the 13th.  Along many reaches, it was the highest stage on the Ohio River since 2005.  Some of the flood gates were installed in the flood walls at Parkersburg, Point Pleasant, and Huntington. ||Many of the flood prone parks and recreational areas along the river were flooded, including some of their outbuildings.  Several roads were closed, causing motorists to take alternate routes for a few days.  ||The unique feature about this flood was the fact that there was no significant headwater river or small stream flooding in West Virginia leading to the flooding along the Ohio River.

The influence of multiple weather features coinciding lead to an average of three to five inches of rain falling over the area. Low pressure moved from Georgia northeast along a cold front across the region while an upper disturbance approached from the Great Lakes region. The combination of these features helped to stall the cold front and its associated precipitation over West Virginia for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in four to six inches of standing water on Cornstalk Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An east to west front sliced through central West Virginia and southern Ohio on the 8th.  A band of rain was seen during the morning along and north of this boundary.  New convection formed during the early afternoon south of the front.  The steering flow was from the northwest at about 35 to 40 mph.  Despite the fast flow, hail was the main element, rather than damaging wind gusts.     ||Behind the first line of convection, a second batch formed in eastern Kentucky and southern West Virginia.  This caused some local training across Lincoln County into Logan and Wyoming Counties into the evening hours.  The town of Logan measured over 2 inches for one of the heaviest amounts.  This rain helped saturate the ground for another rain event, less than 24 hours later, on the 9th.

West Virginia was on the northern fringe of the large severe weather outbreak that occurred across the southern states, northeast into Virginia.  ||A strengthening low pressure center tracked from Arkansas to Ohio.  A strong wind flow exists aloft.  ||During most of the daylight hours on the 27th, the convection remained weak in West Virginia.  As mid and upper level dynamics increased late in the afternoon and early evening, a large area of showers streaked northeast into West Virginia.  Heavier convection was embedded on the southeastern inflow flank of this large area of rain.  This affected the mountainous counties.||Wind damage was common in Raleigh County into Randolph County.  The most damage was in the city of Elkins from straight line winds.  A short lived tornado was confirmed in Nicholas County. ||Due to the wet ground, stream flooding was a concern.  Strong rises occurred on streams and headwater rivers, but luckily no significant flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down near the Intersection of Routes 250 and 28.

Showers and thunderstorms moved north during the overnight hours, ahead of an approaching cold front.  Just prior to the frontal passage, training of showers affected southern Wayne County on north through parts of Lincoln, Putnam and Jackson Counties from 0600E to 0900E on Saturday the 16th.   Rain amounts of 1 to 1.5 inches occurred there in 3 to 4 hours.  This enhanced the rain totals to around 2 inches in that narrow band. ||Minor flash flooding occurred under the repetitive showers during the morning.  Though, one elderly man drove into high water in Jackson County and drowned.  No homes were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams overflowed onto roads.

A mesoscale convective complex moved southeast out of Indiana across eastern Kentucky and into southern West Virginia.  With ample moisture, instability, and lift, new storms formed ahead of the initial complex.  Several of the leading cells showed signs of mid level rotation, but that rotation did not translate to the lower levels.   ||Some of the thunderstorms moved over the same area hit by heavy rain on the 8th.  Measured rain amounts on this second day were only upwards of an inch in 1 to 2 hours.  Yet, based on weather radar estimates, some rain totals of 1 to 1.5 inches likely fell between 1330 and 1530E.  Due to the extremely wet soils, flash flooding still occurred in the border area of Lincoln and Logan Counties.  This was from the Big Ugly and Harts vicinity of Lincoln County on east into extreme western Logan County, mainly west of Chapmanville.||A state of emergency was declared by Governor Tomblin, mainly to facilitate clean-up and stream restoration work by the state highway department and conservation agency.

A low pressure system, moving from the plains to the Ohio Valley, brought widespread rainfall of 1 to 2 inches over western Pennsylvania beginning Saturday March 5th, and lasting through Sunday morning, March 6th. Area streams and creeks rose in response to this rainfall causing localized flooding in Venango and Forest counties. Rain then transitioned to snow Sunday with snow totals of 7 to 10 inches over the mountains in Garrett and Preston counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The influence of multiple weather features coinciding lead to an average of three to five inches of rain falling over the area. Low pressure moved from Georgia northeast along a cold front across the region while an upper disturbance approached from the Great Lakes region. The combination of these features helped to stall the cold front and its associated precipitation over West Virginia for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain prompted the flooding of a stream that inundated Low Water Bridge on Pembroke Road by a depth of six inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An east to west front sliced through central West Virginia and southern Ohio on the 8th.  A band of rain was seen during the morning along and north of this boundary.  New convection formed during the early afternoon south of the front.  The steering flow was from the northwest at about 35 to 40 mph.  Despite the fast flow, hail was the main element, rather than damaging wind gusts.     ||Behind the first line of convection, a second batch formed in eastern Kentucky and southern West Virginia.  This caused some local training across Lincoln County into Logan and Wyoming Counties into the evening hours.  The town of Logan measured over 2 inches for one of the heaviest amounts.  This rain helped saturate the ground for another rain event, less than 24 hours later, on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex moved southeast out of Indiana across eastern Kentucky and into southern West Virginia.  With ample moisture, instability, and lift, new storms formed ahead of the initial complex.  Several of the leading cells showed signs of mid level rotation, but that rotation did not translate to the lower levels.   ||Some of the thunderstorms moved over the same area hit by heavy rain on the 8th.  Measured rain amounts on this second day were only upwards of an inch in 1 to 2 hours.  Yet, based on weather radar estimates, some rain totals of 1 to 1.5 inches likely fell between 1330 and 1530E.  Due to the extremely wet soils, flash flooding still occurred in the border area of Lincoln and Logan Counties.  This was from the Big Ugly and Harts vicinity of Lincoln County on east into extreme western Logan County, mainly west of Chapmanville.||A state of emergency was declared by Governor Tomblin, mainly to facilitate clean-up and stream restoration work by the state highway department and conservation agency.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind flow existed ahead of a cold front.  Thunderstorms were racing northeast at speeds over 60 mph.||The strongest storms moved from Wayne and Lincoln Counties on through Kanawha County to Braxton County.  Wind damage was most widespread in the urban areas around Charleston.  A wind gust to 60 mph was measured at the National Weather Service Office in the Southridge section of Charleston.  A gust of around 70 mph was estimated from Flatwoods of Braxton County.  Numerous trees or large branches were blown down, some onto power lines and houses.  In Kanawha County, 29,000 customers were without power.  Yeager Airport generator failed, causing flights to be delayed and diverted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to ten large trees were uprooted along Coonskin Drive.  One fallen tree damaged the metal roof to a business.  One house was slightly damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure produced strong southeast winds ahead of an approaching cold front along and east of the ridges of Garrett county Maryland, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania with numerous reports of trees and power lines down. Winds across this area gusted to over 60 MPH, with the highest gust of 63 MPH recorded at the Latrobe airport in Westmoreland county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The most serious damage was within the city limits.  Damage was consistent with straight line winds.  Seventeen structures were damaged to some degree.  The damage ranged from trees blown into homes and asphalt shingles blown off roofs, to the entire roof torn off the Veterans of Foreign Wars building.  ||Maximum wind gusts were estimated at around 70 mph.

Access in and out of some of the hollows was limited.    ||One mobile home was swept off its foundation by a mud and debris slide.  Two other homes had moderate flood damage.   There were no injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

A complex area of low pressure tracked west of the Appalachians on the 25th and a cold front pushed across the mountains during the late morning. Winds behind the front were extremely gusty across much of the area. Damage was to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down by high winds on power lines near Rainelle causing numerous power outages. A wind gust to 46 mph was recorded at 1121 AM at Greenbrier Valley Airport (KLWB) AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms moved north during the overnight hours, ahead of an approaching cold front.  Just prior to the frontal passage, training of showers affected southern Wayne County on north through parts of Lincoln, Putnam and Jackson Counties from 0600E to 0900E on Saturday the 16th.   Rain amounts of 1 to 1.5 inches occurred there in 3 to 4 hours.  This enhanced the rain totals to around 2 inches in that narrow band. ||Minor flash flooding occurred under the repetitive showers during the morning.  Though, one elderly man drove into high water in Jackson County and drowned.  No homes were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams overflowed onto the usual low spots.  Shortly after 0900E, a 79 year old man was heading back home after a church pancake breakfast.  He was traveling down Copper Fork Road near Sandyville which is also Route 33/8.  Water from Copper Fork had come out of the banks in an area considered a low water crossing.  The man still tried to drive across the dip in the road.  The water was moving swiftly and his car was pulled into the creek.  Before emergency workers reached the scene, neighbors had successfully pulled him halfway out of the car with a rope.  At that time, the vehicle was pushed further down the stream.  The car became lodged under a bridge and the neighbors lost sight of the driver.  Swift water rescuers could not save him.  He drowned.

Thunderstorms were racing northeast at speeds over 60 mph.||The strongest storms moved from Wayne and Lincoln Counties on through Kanawha County to Braxton County.  Wind damage was most widespread in the urban areas around Charleston.  A wind gust to 60 mph was measured at the National Weather Service Office in the Southridge section of Charleston.  A gust of around 70 mph was estimated from Flatwoods of Braxton County.  Numerous trees or large branches were blown down, some onto power lines and houses.  In Kanawha County, 29,000 customers were without power.  Yeager Airport generator failed, causing flights to be delayed and diverted.

A strong and closed low pressure system over the Ohio Valley remained nearly stationary through the 16th, bringing copious moisture northward up the eastern seaboard. Lift to produce showers and thunderstorms was focused along a warm front that passed north through the mid-Atlantic during the morning. With ground already saturated from several rounds of significant rainfall in the past week, flooding and flash flooding resulted. Rainfall amounts over the eastern West Virginia panhandle exceeded 3 inches in spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide and flooded basements were reported near the top of the mountain near the Virginia border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well defined squall line raced northeast up the Ohio Valley during the afternoon, reaching western West Virginia in the evening, before weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell on electrical power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front slowly passed through the region during the morning of the 28th   A southerly flow ahead of the front caused deep moisture over the area. The cold front combined with the instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall rates. Slow moving and training convection combined to produce flash flooding in some locations, with rainfall measurements approaching 1.5 in places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported on Jordan Run Road along the north fork of Lunice Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Predawn convection was in northern and eastern Ohio, along a slowly moving warm front.  The convection dropped southeast around the eastern side of the mid level ridge. Storms pulsed stronger, causing some large hail.  ||A narrow ribbon of repetitive showers and thunderstorms occurred in southern Tyler County, along the county line of Lewis and Braxton Counties, into northern Webster County.  Rain of 1.5 to 2 inches in less than 2 hours caused minor flash flooding.

A strong upper level low pressure system and cold front moved across the region on the night of the 4th into early on the 5th of April. A line of showers and thunderstorms accompanied this front producing isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down on Lockbridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repetitive showers and thunderstorms, moved southeast through southeast Ohio and into portions of northeast Kentucky, and western West Virginia between 1500E and 1900E on Tuesday, the 10th. ||This convection was just northeast of a warm front.  A sharp dew point gradient existed across the front.  Surface dew points were around 70 degrees just southwest of the boundary. ||Initially, the main impact was large hail.  As back building caused repetitive showers and thunderstorms, flooding became the primary issue.  Maximum rain amounts of 2.5 to 3 inches were observed in portions of Wayne and Cabell Counties.  The Huntington airport measured 2.78 inches of rain.  The cooperative observer in West Huntington had 2.58 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down throughout the community.  Extensive power outages and scattered damage to structures occurred.  The damage also crossed the river to Louisa in Kentucky.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility was around one quarter of a mile at Petersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure centered over New England pushed a back door cold front into Southeast West Virginia. This front, combined with an upper level low pressure system moving through the region, triggered severe thunderstorms. Some of these storms produced large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid level dynamics overcame low buoyancy to reform showers in southern Ohio during the late afternoon.  The showers were efficient rain producers as they moved east into West Virginia.  A small area of repetitive showers occurred over southern Wood County, between Rockport and Mineral Wells.  Rain amounts of 2.5 to 3 inches, in just over 2 hours, were likely over portions of the Tygart and Slate Creek basins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tygart Creek flooded portions of Route 21 and adjacent Interstate 77.  A foot of water occurred in spots along the interstate between the rest stop and the Mineral Wells exit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An east to west oriented cold front sagged south into eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia during the early evening on the 9th.  Temperatures reached the upper 80s with dew points near 70.  There was very little convection over northern West Virginia at 1900E.  Near sunset, with an approaching disturbance in the winds aloft, convection fired in north central West Virginia, with new cells developing to the west during the evening.  Large hail and severe wind gusts occurred in a few locations.||Repetitive showers and the intensity of the rain did cause some local heavier rain amounts.  Small stream and urban flooding occurred in Harrison and Gilmer Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Horn Creek and its feeder runs flooded roads around Coxs Mills.  A water rescue occurred when water surrounded a house, barn, and vehicle in the Tanner Creek basin.

However, no homes were flooded according to county and state officials.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather impacted the area. A strong upper level trough approaching from the west provided strong winds aloft, and a weak upper level low pressure system moved across during the afternoon hours setting off scattered thunderstorms. These storms quickly became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down near Bingham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms developed along a warm front that stretched from Indiana southeast into the Carolinas. An upper level disturbance helped to trigger the storms in the mid-afternoon over southeast West Virginia. Hail up to 1.5 inches fell from the stronger thunderstorm cores. In addition the hail persisted up to 30 minutes in some locations and became quite deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was deep enough to shovel near Sandstone.

Southerly winds in advance of a cold front ushered in low level moisture. Warm temperatures combined with this moisture to produce enough instability to trigger isolated severe thunderstorms. One of these storms brought damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds. Four homes also received damage to their siding and shingles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface boundary rotated northeast through the area during the afternoon of the 14th.  Short lines of thunderstorms developed in the afternoon heating, as clouds broke.  Some repetitive showers caused narrow bands of heavy rain to fall.  One such band of heavy rain was over northern Wayne County.  Rains of 1.5 to 2.5 inches likely fell in 90 minutes or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams flooded roads and yards.   Lynn Creek and Newcomb Creek were 2 such streams that flooded.  Small private culverts were washed out or damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that originally developed over the western Carolinas, intensified and moved northeastward towards the Delmarva peninsula on the 7th. While the precipitation gradient remained fairly tight, the system did move far enough west to impact the higher elevations of West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and the ridges in Western Pennsylvania. Eight to twelve inches of heavy wet snow fell over Preston, Tucker, and Garrett counties while Three to five inches of snow fell in the higher ridge tops in Westmoreland and Fayette counties. Little snow accumulated further west. The system quickly moved off to the east late Wednesday night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Out ahead of a strong late winter cold front, dew points rose into the 50s.  A  marginally unstable air mass allowed a squall line to sweep through during the morning hours.  Hail and strong wind gusts were associated with the convection.  Electrical power was interrupted in many counties. ||In the wake of the squall line, light to moderate rain lingered into the afternoon.  Rain amounts of around 1.5 inches in 24 hours over wet ground, caused a few streams to overflow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust was estimated at around 70 mph.  Trees were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified was located off the New England Coast while high pressure approached from the Ohio Valley. The pressure gradient between these two systems caused breezy conditions. Cold temperatures combined with breezy conditions for extremely low wind chill values near the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values were estimated to be around -20F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds behind a developing low pressure moving off the Mid Atlantic coast allowed for persistent upslope snow showers across the southeast West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers, heavy at times, led to significant accumulations generally from the evening of the 11th through the night of the 12th. Total accumulations of up to 8 inches across the western part of the county were observed. Winds gusting from 20 to 30 miles per hour reduced visibility to a mile or two for much of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds behind a developing low pressure moving off the Mid Atlantic coast allowed for persistent upslope snow showers across the southeast West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers, heavy at times, led to significant accumulations generally from the evening of the 11th through the night of the 12th. Total accumulations of up to 15 inches across the western part of the county were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain amounts of over an inch were common late on Saturday the 5th.  A strong cold front came through during the predawn hours of Sunday the 6th.  A few hours in the wake of the surface front, the rain changed to wet snow.  Snow accumulations were highly dependent on elevations.  Snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were common, except 1 to 3 inches in McDowell and Wyoming Counties.  The higher terrain around Richwood, Webster Springs, Pickens, and Snowshoe saw 4 to 6 inches of accumulation.

A low pressure system moved from the Gulf of Mexico to the mid-atlantic coast spreading light snow and sleet over the Ohio valley. Most locations received only light snow accumulations during the 24 hour period but warnings were issued for the higher elevation counties of Preston, Tucker, and Garrett. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 7 to 9 inches of snow over the warned area.

A low pressure system moved from the Gulf of Mexico to the mid-atlantic coast spreading light snow and sleet over the Ohio valley. Most locations received only light snow accumulations during the 24 hour period but warnings were issued for the higher elevation counties of Preston, Tucker, and Garrett. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 7 to 9 inches of snow over the warned area.

A complex weather system moved through the region on Wednesday the 26th. An area of low pressure developed off the North Carolina coast, while an upper level low passed overhead. Temperatures were initially warm enough for some rain, sleet and freezing rain, however the precipitation quickly changed to wet snow. Enough instability was present under the upper level low for the snow to take on a banded form for much of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a heavy wet snow by mid morning on Wednesday the 26th, resulting in 4 to 6 inches of snow across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary located across eastern West Virginia on the 23rd. An upper-level disturbance tracked through the area during this time, bringing showers and thunderstorms. A few of the thunderstorms became severe due to limited instability near the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down near Slanesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain amounts of over an inch were common late on Saturday the 5th.  A strong cold front came through during the predawn hours of Sunday the 6th.  A few hours in the wake of the surface front, the rain changed to wet snow.  Snow accumulations were highly dependent on elevations.  Snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were common, except 1 to 3 inches in McDowell and Wyoming Counties.  The higher terrain around Richwood, Webster Springs, Pickens, and Snowshoe saw 4 to 6 inches of accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northeast from Missouri towards the Ohio Valley in the afternoon on January 11th, bringing light to moderate snow to eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland. As the low pressure system over the Ohio Valley moved eastward and weakened, another low pressure system intensified along the coast of Long Island, NY bringing additional upslope heavy snow to the higher elevations of northern West Virginia and Maryland  and the on the ridges of Fayette County in Pennsylvania on January 12th and 13th. 15-17 inches of snowfall were recorded in Terra Alta, Preston County and Davis, Tucker County over the 48 hour period and 11.2 was recorded in a 24 hour period in Fayette County.

A complex weather system moved through the region on Wednesday the 26th. An area of low pressure developed off the North Carolina coast, while an upper level low passed overhead. Temperatures were initially warm enough for some rain, sleet and freezing rain, however the precipitation quickly changed to wet snow. Enough instability was present under the upper level low for the snow to take on a banded form for much of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a heavy wet snow by mid morning on Wednesday the 26th, resulting in 2 to 7 inches of snow across the county. The highest amounts were across the higher elevations, as warm surface temperatures kept accumulations down across the lower elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through the region on the evening of the 21st. High pressure to the north allowed for plenty of cold air to move into the region during the nighttime hours of the 21st. A wave of low pressure was moving along the front during this time causing precipitation over the area. Precipitation initially started out as rain before changing over to ice and snow as colder air worked its way in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet totaled up to 1.3 inches at Bayard. A glaze of ice accumulation from freezing rain was reported near Kline Gap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified off the New England coast while high pressure approached from the Midwest. A strong gradient between these two systems caused windy conditions. The gusty winds combined with cold air for extremely low wind chills for locations near the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills were estimated to be around -20F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast flow aloft existed south of a cold front.  Surface dew points were in the low and mid 50s.  Temperatures reached into the 70s south of the front.  Showers and thunderstorms formed.  Hail was common, with a few reports of large hail.  Surprisingly, strong wind gusts were not an issue.

A strong upper level low pressure spinning over the eastern Great Lakes pushed several disturbances across southeast West Virginia. These disturbances combined with persistent northwest flow led to accumulating snow showers across the higher elevations from the 7th into the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers fell heaviest on Saturday the 8th across the county. Amounts ranged from just a couple inches across the lower elevations, to 7 inches in Bluefield, and up to 11 inches across the higher western facing slopes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds behind a developing low pressure moving off the Mid Atlantic coast allowed for persistent upslope snow showers across the southeast West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers, heavy at times, led to significant accumulations generally from the evening of the 11th through the night of the 12th. Total accumulations of up to 8 inches across the western part of the county were observed. Winds gusting from 20 to 30 miles per hour reduced visibility to a mile or two for much of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed on the afternoon of May 26th in a very unstable air mass across southeast West Virginia. These storms were primarily large hail producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell near Valley Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed on the afternoon of May 26th in a very unstable air mass across southeast West Virginia. These storms were primarily large hail producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to half-dollar size was reported along Little Creek Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail report from 108 Bruce Allen Road south of Hilldale.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bluefield ASOS had a measured wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed on the afternoon of May 26th in a very unstable air mass across southeast West Virginia. These storms were primarily large hail producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were brought down by thunderstorm winds on Red Sulphur Turnpke near Concord University.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed on the afternoon of May 26th in a very unstable air mass across southeast West Virginia. These storms were primarily large hail producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail was reported in and around Lewisburg from a variety of sources. Hail sizes ranged from about 1 to 2 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed on the afternoon of May 26th in a very unstable air mass across southeast West Virginia. These storms were primarily large hail producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees downed by thunderstorm winds along Route 20 near Lily Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms developed along a warm front that stretched from Indiana southeast into the Carolinas. An upper level disturbance helped to trigger the storms in the mid-afternoon over southeast West Virginia. Hail up to 1.5 inches fell from the stronger thunderstorm cores. In addition the hail persisted up to 30 minutes in some locations and became quite deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 20 to 30 minutes of hail just under golf ball size on Sweet Springs Valley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system, and it's associated cold front crossed through the region late in the day on the 26th. Scattered severe thunderstorms, most of which produced wind damage, developed along the frontal boundary in an area of instability and high shear. A microburst occurred in Monongalia county in West Virginia with an estimated wind speed of 70-80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey team confirms a microburst/straight line wind damage near Westover in Monongalia county, West Virginia. A severe thunderstorm rolled through the Morgantown area on Thursday afternoon. The storm produced a microburst with winds between 70 to 80 mph. Numerous trees came down in the Westover area. A metal roof from a car dealership was blown off in downtown Morgantown, while trees were uprooted on the campus of West Virginia University. The strong winds knocked down or uprooted trees sporadically eastward until the storm reached the Pleasant Hill Cemetery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system approaching from the west helped to trigger numerous showers and thunderstorms. The storms developed along a slow-moving frontal boundary, and with abundant moisture in place, some of the storms produced heavy rainfall with flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains of 1 to 1.5 inches in a few hours caused water to run over a road in Pinnacle Rock State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Canadian high pressure build over eastern West Virginia during the early morning hours of the 5th. Mainly clear skies along with light winds allowed temperatures to drop below freezing during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were estimated to be in the lower 20s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a weak slow moving stationary front over Ohio. As was the case the days prior, storms were slow-moving causing flooding in northwestern Pennsylvania. Some wind damage was reported with the storms as they moved through the panhandle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports trees down in cemetary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a weak slow moving stationary front over Ohio. As was the case the days prior, storms were slow-moving causing flooding in northwestern Pennsylvania. Some wind damage was reported with the storms as they moved through the panhandle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcaster reports 39 trees down at the Oglebay resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed upper level low pressure moved across the Ohio valley on the 15th into the 16th. In advance of the system, strong southeast winds produced wind damage across the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six power lines were blown down by high winds throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary located across eastern West Virginia on the 23rd. An upper-level disturbance tracked through the area during this time, bringing showers and thunderstorms. A few of the thunderstorms became severe due to limited instability near the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down over a road in Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved across the region, numerous showers and thunderstorms developed along it.  Some of the storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree down on Teaberry Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved across the region, numerous showers and thunderstorms developed along it.  Some of the storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds and hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system tracked along a warm front across Ohio and Pennsylvania severe thunderstorms developed just south of the front in Ohio and progressed eastward. Large hail was reported with many of the storms, with hail up to the size of golf balls reported in both Ohio and Pennsylvania. A strong supercell thunderstorm produced an EF-2 tornado that crossed through Westmoreland county in Pennsylvania during rush hour. Around 90 homes sustained damage in Westmoreland county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe squall line that developed ahead of a cold front moved across eastern Ohio, the northern West Virginia Panhandle, and western Pennsylvania in the early morning hours of April 20th. Wind damage was widespread with NWS surveys confirming 2 seperate microbursts in Muskingum and Tuscarawas counties with total damages over 1 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported trees and powerlines down near Colliers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system tracked along a warm front across Ohio and Pennsylvania severe thunderstorms developed just south of the front in Ohio and progressed eastward. Large hail was reported with many of the storms, with hail up to the size of golf balls reported in both Ohio and Pennsylvania. A strong supercell thunderstorm produced an EF-2 tornado that crossed through Westmoreland county in Pennsylvania during rush hour. Around 90 homes sustained damage in Westmoreland county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reports several trees down in Parsons along route 72.

Snow developed during the day on Tuesday the 11th.  This was in response to a weak low pressure tracking east across the upper Ohio River Valley.  The snow intensity increased during the late evening hours of the 11th, then transitioned into snow showers during the overnight.  The snow showers became drier and fluffier as colder air blew in during the early morning hours of the 12th.  The snow showers decreased in coverage and intensity by dawn on the 13th.||Increases in snow depth of a foot were common across Upshur, Randolph, Webster, and western Pocahontas Counties.  The snow depth increased 6 to 10 inches across Barbour, Nicholas, Fayette, Raleigh, Wyoming, and McDowell Counties.  For example, some preliminary data indicated the snow depth in the town of Buckhannon went from 3 inches to 15 inches.  On the mountaintop near Richwood, the snow depth increased from 5 inches to 21 inches. The snow depth at Pickens increased from 10 inches prior to this event to 24 inches.  A few other increases in snow depth included Beckley from 4 inches to 9 inches, Elkins from 5 inches to 12 inches, Oak Hill from 2 inches to 9 inches, Belington from 3 inches to 10 inches, Oceana from zero to 8 inches, and finally Mt Nebo from 4 inches to 16 inches.||Lesser amounts of snow fell across the western lowland counties.  Snow accumulations of 4 to 6 inches were common across Calhoun, Roane, Jackson, Braxton, Gilmer, Lewis, Harrison, and Taylor Counties.  However, some narrow banding of the snow showers occurred during the evening hours of the 12th, causing localized deeper snow accumulations.

Rain fell from the 6th into the morning of the 7th.  After 1 to 2 inches of rain, the precipitation changed to wet snow during the early afternoon hours of the 7th across the mountainous counties.  A 2 to 5 inch snow accumulation was common in about 6 hours.  Amounts were highly dependent on elevation.  A maximum snow accumulation of 5 to 7 inches occurred near Pickens, Glady, and Harman of Randolph County.  The snow quickly diminished after sunset, as the strong mid and upper level dynamics raced northeast.   ||Due to the wet nature of the snow, a few power outages did occur.

Low pressure tracked through the Ohio Valley on the 11th before transferring its energy to another area of low pressure off the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the evening hours. A period of snow associated with these systems affected the area during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 11th. Upslope snow continued into the early morning hours of the 12 for locations along and west of the Allegheny front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 1.8 inches about two miles southwest of Keyser.

A slow moving upper level low over the central U.S provided for southerly flow and moisture transport to eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. Showers and thunderstorms with heavy downpours developed in an area out ahead of a cold front allowing for training and rainfall amounts of over 2 inches in some locations. Some flooding was reported along creeks, streams, and in low lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported roads covered up to one foot deep from small creeks.

A cold rain pushed northeast into West Virginia during the Wednesday evening the 25th.  A strong mid and upper level disturbance approached by dawn on Thursday the 26th.  In response to the dynamics aloft, a weak surface low developed to the south of West Virginia, while the intensity and coverage of the rain increased.  The rain changed to wet snow between dawn and late morning.  The heaviest snow fell between midday and 1600E.  Elkins measured 7 to 8 inches of snow between 1300E and 1900E.  As the strong dynamics aloft shifted east, the snow quickly decreased in intensity after dark.   Snow accumulations of 5 to 8 inches were common in less than 12 hours.  Pickens in Randolph County had the most snow reported with 10 inches.||The heavy wet snow caused some electrical outages.  Several thousand customers were without power.  The most outages were reported in Upshur, Barbour, Randolph, and Taylor Counties.

A low pressure system that originally developed over the western Carolinas, intensified and moved northeastward towards the Delmarva peninsula on the 7th. While the precipitation gradient remained fairly tight, the system did move far enough west to impact the higher elevations of West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and the ridges in Western Pennsylvania. Eight to twelve inches of heavy wet snow fell over Preston, Tucker, and Garrett counties while Three to five inches of snow fell in the higher ridge tops in Westmoreland and Fayette counties. Little snow accumulated further west. The system quickly moved off to the east late Wednesday night.

This was a synoptic scale event.  A strong north to south temperature gradient existed along the Interstate 70 corridor in Ohio.  Low pressure moved out of Illinois in the morning, reaching western Pennsylvania by evening.||Individual thunderstorm cells developed in western Ohio and southern Indiana around midday, reaching into West Virginia during the late afternoon and evening.  Hail was initially the main impact, but stronger wind gusts developed as the thunderstorms|matured and consolidated.| |Out ahead, a separate area of showers and thunderstorms bushed southern West Virginia during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto power lines along Rose Lane Drive in Mink Shoals.

The rain changed to wet snow between dawn and late morning.  The heaviest snow fell between midday and 1600E.  Elkins measured 7 to 8 inches of snow between 1300E and 1900E.  As the strong dynamics aloft shifted east, the snow quickly decreased in intensity after dark.   Snow accumulations of 5 to 8 inches were common in less than 12 hours.  Pickens in Randolph County had the most snow reported with 10 inches.||The heavy wet snow caused some electrical outages.  Several thousand customers were without power.  The most outages were reported in Upshur, Barbour, Randolph, and Taylor Counties.

As powerful low pressure moved up the Mid Atlantic seaboard heavy wet snow fell over the higher elevations of Northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and Western Pennsylvania. Storm totals of 6 to 12 inches of snow fell in 12 hours with some trees and power lines down in parts of Preston county West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland.

Low pressure tracked through the Ohio Valley on the 11th before transferring its energy to another area of low pressure off the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the evening hours. A period of snow associated with these systems affected the area during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 11th. Upslope snow continued into the early morning hours of the 12 for locations along and west of the Allegheny front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to four inches in Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the Ohio Valley on the 11th before transferring its energy to another area of low pressure off the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the evening hours. A period of snow associated with these systems affected the area during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 11th. Upslope snow continued into the early morning hours of the 12 for locations along and west of the Allegheny front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was estimated to be between two and four inches across western Pendleton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the day on Tuesday the 11th.  This was in response to a weak low pressure tracking east across the upper Ohio River Valley.  The snow intensity increased during the late evening hours of the 11th, then transitioned into snow showers during the overnight.  The snow showers became drier and fluffier as colder air blew in during the early morning hours of the 12th.  The snow showers decreased in coverage and intensity by dawn on the 13th.||Increases in snow depth of a foot were common across Upshur, Randolph, Webster, and western Pocahontas Counties.  The snow depth increased 6 to 10 inches across Barbour, Nicholas, Fayette, Raleigh, Wyoming, and McDowell Counties.  For example, some preliminary data indicated the snow depth in the town of Buckhannon went from 3 inches to 15 inches.  On the mountaintop near Richwood, the snow depth increased from 5 inches to 21 inches. The snow depth at Pickens increased from 10 inches prior to this event to 24 inches.  A few other increases in snow depth included Beckley from 4 inches to 9 inches, Elkins from 5 inches to 12 inches, Oak Hill from 2 inches to 9 inches, Belington from 3 inches to 10 inches, Oceana from zero to 8 inches, and finally Mt Nebo from 4 inches to 16 inches.||Lesser amounts of snow fell across the western lowland counties.  Snow accumulations of 4 to 6 inches were common across Calhoun, Roane, Jackson, Braxton, Gilmer, Lewis, Harrison, and Taylor Counties.  However, some narrow banding of the snow showers occurred during the evening hours of the 12th, causing localized deeper snow accumulations.

A strong upper level low pressure spinning over the eastern Great Lakes pushed several disturbances across southeast West Virginia. These disturbances combined with persistent northwest flow led to accumulating snow showers across the higher elevations from the 7th into the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow showers across the western part of the county resulted in up to a foot of snow. While locations across the lower elevations in the eastern part of the county saw only an inch or two.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain fell from the 6th into the morning of the 7th.  After 1 to 2 inches of rain, the precipitation changed to wet snow during the early afternoon hours of the 7th across the mountainous counties.  A 2 to 5 inch snow accumulation was common in about 6 hours.  Amounts were highly dependent on elevation.  A maximum snow accumulation of 5 to 7 inches occurred near Pickens, Glady, and Harman of Randolph County.  The snow quickly diminished after sunset, as the strong mid and upper level dynamics raced northeast.   ||Due to the wet nature of the snow, a few power outages did occur.

Upper-level high pressure provided enough subsidence to cause hot conditions on the 21st. Surface high pressure over the Atlantic caused moist air to move into the region from the south. The combination of the heat and humidity caused heat indices over 105 degrees in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices around 105 degrees were estimated due to observations nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level high pressure provided enough subsidence to cause hot conditions on the 21st. Surface high pressure over the Atlantic caused moist air to move into the region from the south. The combination of the heat and humidity caused heat indices over 105 degrees in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices around 105 degrees were estimated due to observations nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through eastern West Virginia during the morning hours of the 7th. An upper-level low moved into the region from the Tennessee Valley causing surface low pressure to develop along the front. Low pressure allowed for precipitation to continue on the cold side of the front during the afternoon hours of the 7th lasting into the early morning hours of the 8th. There was enough cold air to cause precipitation to end as snow during this time. ||The highest snowfall amounts were along the Allegheny front of eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was estimated to be around 6.0 inches across western Mineral County.
