EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 6.0 inches at Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over New England pumped in an abundant amount of cold air while low pressure developed along the Mid-Atlantic Coast. Precipitation well ahead of the low overspread eastern West Virginia during the evening hours of the 28th. Precipitation continued into the 29th before ending during the afternoon hours. There was enough cold air for precipitation to fall in the form of snow especially across the Potomac Highlands. ||The combination of heavy wet snow on trees that still had leaves caused numerous power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 8.5 inches at Lost River and 6.0 inches at Mathias.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms across eastern West Virginia. Moderate instability ahead of the front caused an isolated thunderstorm to produce damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The ground was wetter than normal from around an inch of rain that occurred on the 15th into the 16th. Periods of rain started to fall again on Sunday the 20th.  A front then sank south on Monday the 21st and became nearly stationary just south of Huntington and Charleston.  Rain fell heaviest just north of that boundary late Monday afternoon into the early evening across central West Virginia.  From near Jackson County on east to southern Upshur County about a half inch to three quarters of an inch of rain fell.     ||On Tuesday, the 22nd, there were 2 distinct waves of rain.  The first wave was from about 0600E to 1300E as the stationary front was beginning to lift back north as a warm front.  A lull then occurred during the mid afternoon.  The second wave of rain occurred from around 1800E to 0200E on Wednesday the 23rd.  This second wave was associated with the moisture plume ahead of the cold front.  Dew points were on either side of 60 degrees in eastern Kentucky and Tennessee.  The heaviest rain rates from both these waves were on the order of three quarters of an inch in 3 hours.  The total 24 hour rain amounts ending at dawn on the 23rd were over 2 inches from eastern Putnam County and southern Jackson County, through northern Kanawha County, Roane, and Calhoun Counties, and into portions of Clay and Gilmer Counties.     ||The axis of the maximum 3 day rain amount went from near Huntington on northeast through northern Putnam County into Roane County to southern Upshur County.   The 3 day rain total of 2.5 to 3.5 inches was common.  The heaviest recorded rain amount was from the cooperative observer at Spencer, where 4.6 inches was measured.||Widespread small stream flooding started Tuesday morning.  Much of the small stream flooding continued overnight Tuesday night with the second wave of rain.  The larger headwater rivers, including the Pocatalico, the West Fork, the Buckhannon, and the Little Kanawha, then caused minor flooding to continue through Wednesday the 23rd.  The main effect of both the small stream and the river flooding was to close the usual low vulnerable spots on adjacent roads.  As a result, citizens had limited access.  Several vehicles were flooded because drivers tried to drive through water that was too deep.  Being deer hunting season, several hunters found themselves unable to cross swollen streams.  Vulnerable parks and camping spots along the rivers were also flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork River crested at, or slightly over flood stage, around midday on the 23rd.  The crest at the Clarksburg gauge was 14.36 feet.  Flood stage is 14 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly clear skies along with light winds provided a good setup for radiational cooling. There was enough low-level moisture for areas of dense fog to develop across portions of eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility was measured around one quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex headed south through Kentucky. The eastern extent of the system helped trigger additional thunderstorms over parts of West Virginia. Severe thunderstorm wind accompanied one of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew trees down in Camp Creek State Park. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of the 21st, a thunderstorm complex headed south through eastern Kentucky. Convection along the eastern edge of this system helped to prompt additional development farther east.  This process repeated itself through the evening hours leading to multiple thunderstorms moving through the region, with many of the storms producing damaging wind or large hail. Flash flooding also occurred with some of the slower moving storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind downed multiple large trees near the intersection of Stringtown Road and Route 219.  Stringtown Road was blocked by the downed trees. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite being under the influence by an area of high pressure, enough ridge top convergence of winds took place to help facilitate the development of isolated to scattered thunderstorms. One of these storms reached severe levels over southeast West Virginia.

A cold front swept through the region on the 28th.  Multiple clusters of storms accompanied the front as it progressed. One of these storms increased to severe levels over southeast West Virginia and produced large hail. Another storm produced enough rain in a short period of time to cause flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging in size from dime to quarter size fell for a 15 minute period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite being under the influence by an area of high pressure, enough ridge top convergence of winds took place to help facilitate the development of isolated to scattered thunderstorms. One of these storms reached severe levels over southeast West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a couple of large trees down on mountain property. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thermal gradient in the lower levels existed along the Ohio River Valley as a surface front waited for an approaching upper air disturbance.  A few storms formed over the lower Kanawha River Valley.  However, the main cluster of storms formed late in the afternoon across northern Kentucky.  These storms then continued northeast through the late evening hours, reaching into north central West Virginia.

More storms developed during the late afternoon and into the early evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multi-cluster thunderstorms occurred along the outer edge of a flattening ridge over the central plains states through the time period. With ample surface heating, instability and weak shear some of the thunderstorms produced heavy downpours causing localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported of urban-type flooding in Parsons. Small streams and culverts overflowing and effecting vegetation. Some roads closed including Route 219 at the Tucker/Randolph county line. Some homes and local businesses effected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough provided cold air aloft while warm and moist air remained at the surface. Moderate amounts of instability developed during the afternoon and evening hours. An isolated thunderstorm was able to produce damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm drains were ripped from a house due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The hottest day of the year in many localities helped to form afternoon convection over the mountainous counties.  Their outflow boundaries triggered additional thunderstorms during the late afternoon into the evening over the western lowlands.  Some of the storms pulsed briefly to severe limits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid and upper level low pressure system intensified and brought an early snow to the high mountain terrain of Pocahontas, Randolph, and eastern Webster Counties.  The first flakes began during predawn hours of Saturday the 1st at elevations at or above 3000 feet.  The snow continued, in varying intensity, through Sunday the 2nd and ended on Monday morning the 3rd.||The temperature remained below freezing above 4000 feet at Snowshoe during the day on the 1st and again on Sunday the 2nd.  ||Two day snow accumulation of 3 to 6 inches occurred at elevations above 3500 feet, with an inch or two around 3000 feet.

On July 11th an upper level ridge of high pressure was centered just off to our southwest. Upper level storm systems riding along the edge of this ridge generated a few thunderstorm complexes that approached our region. Both complexes tended to weaken as they entered southeast West Virginia. However, the outflow from these systems was strong enough to produce some wind damage in Greenbrier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds from a thunderstorm outflow snapped off several 4 to 6 inch diameter tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well to the east of a cold front in the Ohio Valley, thunderstorms formed by the afternoon on the 4th.  These storms then moved north through West Virginia.  The storms were supported by a strong core of winds aloft over the Great Lakes.   The convection also dumped local downpours.  Minor flash flooding occurred in a few locations.||The remnants of Tropical Storm Lee then rode northeast to enhance and prolong the frontal rains on Labor Day the 5th, into Tuesday the 6th.   Rain totals from the 4th through the 6th were on the order of 2.5 to 4 inches.  However, due to the dry ground leading into this event, only some minor small stream flooding occurred late in the event.

Nocturnal showers and thunderstorms formed in northern Ohio south into northwestern West Virginia during the late evening hours, along the leading edge of a dry intrusion aloft.  The line reached it peak intensity over Harrison County.  Local downpours of 3 to 4 inches occurred in 1 to 2 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe flash flooding occurred along Ann Moore Run.  Its headwaters are around a highly urban shopping mall.   The stream flows through Anmoore and East View.  A lot of the flooding was also from hillside runoff.  One apartment complex on the east side of the shopping center had 16 units damaged.    The fire department in Anmoore was flooded.  One Anmoore resident said, It was the worst one we've had.  Businesses along Philippi Pike were flooded.  ||Flooding of less magnitude spilled over into the Simpson Creek drainage in Bridgeport. ||A total of 10 businesses were affected.  Twenty houses had significant damage.  On the order of 100 homes had water in their basement.  Even the county courthouse had basement flooding.  Damage also occurred to roads and culverts.  On the order of 20 vehicles were flooded.||The cooperative observer in Clarksburg measured 3.3 inches for their 24 hour total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper-level high pressure built over the region during the 22nd. Surface high pressure over the Atlantic Ocean caused moist air to move into the region from the south. Strong subsidence underneath the upper-level high caused hot conditions with air temperatures near degrees. The combination of the heat and high humidity caused heat indices to soar over 105 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices were estimated to be over 105 degrees due to observations nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough, centered over the Ohio valley on the Sept 6th absorbed the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee allowing for deep moisture to slowly progress northward through the Mid-Atlantic states. While most of the persistant heavy rain remained to the east, some flooding occurred along the Appalachians in Northern West Virginia, Garrett County Maryland and Fayette County Pennsylvania along a deformation axis from September 6-7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reports of flooding along Dents Run Road, Kingwood Pike and several roads in the city of Morgantown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing coastal storm off the mid Atlantic coast deposited a 2 to 6 inch accumulation of snow over Randolph and Pocahontas Counties.  These amounts varied depending on elevation.  One to 3 inches of snow fell over Upshur, Webster, and Nicholas Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The ground was wetter than normal from around an inch of rain that occurred on the 15th into the 16th. Periods of rain started to fall again on Sunday the 20th.  A front then sank south on Monday the 21st and became nearly stationary just south of Huntington and Charleston.  Rain fell heaviest just north of that boundary late Monday afternoon into the early evening across central West Virginia.  From near Jackson County on east to southern Upshur County about a half inch to three quarters of an inch of rain fell.     ||On Tuesday, the 22nd, there were 2 distinct waves of rain.  The first wave was from about 0600E to 1300E as the stationary front was beginning to lift back north as a warm front.  A lull then occurred during the mid afternoon.  The second wave of rain occurred from around 1800E to 0200E on Wednesday the 23rd.  This second wave was associated with the moisture plume ahead of the cold front.  Dew points were on either side of 60 degrees in eastern Kentucky and Tennessee.  The heaviest rain rates from both these waves were on the order of three quarters of an inch in 3 hours.  The total 24 hour rain amounts ending at dawn on the 23rd were over 2 inches from eastern Putnam County and southern Jackson County, through northern Kanawha County, Roane, and Calhoun Counties, and into portions of Clay and Gilmer Counties.     ||The axis of the maximum 3 day rain amount went from near Huntington on northeast through northern Putnam County into Roane County to southern Upshur County.   The 3 day rain total of 2.5 to 3.5 inches was common.  The heaviest recorded rain amount was from the cooperative observer at Spencer, where 4.6 inches was measured.||Widespread small stream flooding started Tuesday morning.  Much of the small stream flooding continued overnight Tuesday night with the second wave of rain.  The larger headwater rivers, including the Pocatalico, the West Fork, the Buckhannon, and the Little Kanawha, then caused minor flooding to continue through Wednesday the 23rd.  The main effect of both the small stream and the river flooding was to close the usual low vulnerable spots on adjacent roads.  As a result, citizens had limited access.  Several vehicles were flooded because drivers tried to drive through water that was too deep.  Being deer hunting season, several hunters found themselves unable to cross swollen streams.  Vulnerable parks and camping spots along the rivers were also flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed from small stream flooding on the 22nd.  The Little Kanawha River crested just over flood stage at Elizabeth around sunset on the 23rd.  The crest was 36.34 feet during the late afternoon and early evening.  Flood stage is 36 feet.  Parks and camps along the river were flooded.  At least one recreational vehicle was flooded.

Numerous county roads were closed.  The West Fork of the Little Kanawha rose 21 feet at Rocksdale from around 5.5 feet to a crest near 26.6 feet.  That crest occurred around 0130E on the 23rd.  Some garages and outbuildings along the West Fork of the Little Kanawha River were flooded.  ||The crest at Grantsville on the main stem of the Little Kanawha was just over the 36 foot flood stage, cresting at 36.15 feet around midday on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The ground was wetter than normal from around an inch of rain that occurred on the 15th into the 16th. Periods of rain started to fall again on Sunday the 20th.  A front then sank south on Monday the 21st and became nearly stationary just south of Huntington and Charleston.  Rain fell heaviest just north of that boundary late Monday afternoon into the early evening across central West Virginia.  From near Jackson County on east to southern Upshur County about a half inch to three quarters of an inch of rain fell.     ||On Tuesday, the 22nd, there were 2 distinct waves of rain.  The first wave was from about 0600E to 1300E as the stationary front was beginning to lift back north as a warm front.  A lull then occurred during the mid afternoon.  The second wave of rain occurred from around 1800E to 0200E on Wednesday the 23rd.  This second wave was associated with the moisture plume ahead of the cold front.  Dew points were on either side of 60 degrees in eastern Kentucky and Tennessee.  The heaviest rain rates from both these waves were on the order of three quarters of an inch in 3 hours.  The total 24 hour rain amounts ending at dawn on the 23rd were over 2 inches from eastern Putnam County and southern Jackson County, through northern Kanawha County, Roane, and Calhoun Counties, and into portions of Clay and Gilmer Counties.     ||The axis of the maximum 3 day rain amount went from near Huntington on northeast through northern Putnam County into Roane County to southern Upshur County.   The 3 day rain total of 2.5 to 3.5 inches was common. Much of the small stream flooding continued overnight Tuesday night with the second wave of rain.  The larger headwater rivers, including the Pocatalico, the West Fork, the Buckhannon, and the Little Kanawha, then caused minor flooding to continue through Wednesday the 23rd.  The main effect of both the small stream and the river flooding was to close the usual low vulnerable spots on adjacent roads.  As a result, citizens had limited access. Vulnerable parks and camping spots along the rivers were also flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams around Cross Lanes, Sissonville, and near Clendenin flooded Tuesday.   Streams such as Legg Fork, Tuppers Creek, Derricks Creek, and Haines Branch closed roads on Tuesday.  The Pocatalico River crested at 28.3 feet around dawn on the 23rd, causing road closures into the 23rd. The north bound ramp to Exit 116 of Interstate 77 was closed.   Streams around Clendenin overflow.  One hunter drove his truck into a swollen stream, that was still within its banks.  The vehicle stalled and the 2 occupants escaped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and storms developed by the early afternoon within an area of greater moisture across the central Appalachians. The storms were also fueled by good instability in the lower portion of the atmosphere and plenty of daytime heating. Some of these storms reached severe levels, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down trees along State Route 66. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season snowfall occurred over the highest elevations of Tucker county in West Virginia as an unusually strong and cold upper level disturbance produced 4 to 8 inches of snow over a 48 hour period at Canaan  Valley and Davis.

The heat and humidity levels peaked from Wednesday the 20th into Saturday the 23rd.  High temperatures in the low and mid 90s were common.  Overnight low temperatures only dropped into the low and mid 70s for a few hours near dawn.  Dew points peaked mostly in the mid and upper 70s.  However, Huntington observed an 81 dew point during the evening on the 21st.  The heat index or apparent temperature reached into the 104 to 109 degree range.  Scattered thunderstorms late on the 22nd and on the 23rd helped to dampen the heat for the 24th and 25th, but humidity levels remained high.

In a hot and humid air mass, long lived strong to severe thunderstorms traveled southeast through southeast Ohio during the afternoon and into western West Virginia by late afternoon before weakening.  Gusty winds and frequent lightning were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches were blown down in the vicinity.   Wind gusted to 44 mph at the airport, with similar gusts in the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Predawn thunderstorms formed over Harrison and Lewis Counties and moved through Barbour and Randolph Counties.   The heaviest rains were near the border areas of Barbour, Randolph and Tucker Counties.  Two automatic rain gauges in eastern Barbour County measured around 3.3 inches in about 4 hours.   A spotter in Montrose of Randolph County reported about 3.5 inches.||Later that day, slow moving thunderstorms affected the Barboursville vicinity in Cabell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Leading Creek and its feeder streams flooded roads, fields, and surrounded homes.  Route 219 was closed for a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a front in the Ohio Valley, a large area of showers and thunderstorms moved east, across northern West Virginia during the late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A summer downpour caused street flooding.  During the heaviest period, between 1100E and 1200E, 1.6 inches of rain was measured, with .6 of that hourly amount falling in just 10 minutes.  A nearby spotter measured a total of 2.2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Predawn thunderstorms formed over Harrison and Lewis Counties and moved through Barbour and Randolph Counties. A spotter in Montrose of Randolph County reported about 3.5 inches.||Later that day, slow moving thunderstorms affected the Barboursville vicinity in Cabell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell and damaged the back portion of a house, crushed one vehicle and damaged another.   The associated local downpour also caused water to rush off hillsides and into a garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The heat and humidity caused a very unstable environment during the afternoon.  Slow moving thunderstorms trained over a small portion of Putnam County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downpour of 2.5 to 4 inches in a few hours caused serious street and urban flooding.  A spotter in Winfield reported 3.97 inches of rain for a 24 hour total.   Water damaged a few homes in Winfield.

Thunderstorms developed in the mid to late afternoon generally south of the Interstate 64 corridor.  With the weak flow aloft, the weaker storms rained themselves out.   A stronger elongated cluster of storms developed across Lawrence County Kentucky into southern Wayne County.  Storms trained over a portion of southern Wayne County.  New storms then formed on the northern and northwestern fringes of this weakening cluster.  The new elongated cluster affected Boyd County in Kentucky and northern Wayne County in West Virginia.  More downpours occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams flooded roads and fields.

Scattered thunderstorms late on the 22nd and on the 23rd helped to dampen the heat for the 24th and 25th, but humidity levels remained high.

A developing area of showers and thunderstorms pulsed stronger through the Huntington vicinity.  Rainfall of 1.5 to 2 inches was measured in a hour between 0200E and 0300E.  The street viaducts in the city flooded.  A few vehicles drove into the water and stalled.

A complex of thunderstorms moved out of eastern Kentucky and entered West Virginia. While most of the activity dissipated as it moved through the state, some isolated severe weather managed to arrive across parts of Greenbrier County in the form of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down four or five large limbs that blocked a few roads around Lewisburg. Damage values are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a large limb down on a car on Tuckahoe Road. Damage values are estimated.

Several more rounds of showers and thunderstorms were seen from Friday the 22nd into Sunday the 24th,  Luckily, the heaviest rains remained further west, across southern Ohio and northern Kentucky.  ||Yet, the thunderstorms on Saturday the 23rd did cause wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two Trees were blown down.  One of the trees damaged the roof to a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the warm sector, south and east of a nearly stationary front, one cluster of thunderstorms formed late in the day across southwest Virginia into southern West Virginia.  Later in the evening, the outflow boundary helped formed isolated convection further west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system, originating from the southern plains, moved towards the western Great Lakes on Wednesday March 9th. A wintry mix of precipitation changed over to all rain as the warm front associated with this system pushed across the region on Wednesday morning. Periods of moderate to heavy rain continued through the passage of the upper level trough late Thursday. With much of the region already saturated from previous rainfall over the past 2 weeks many streams and creeks were already elevated. The additional 1 to 2 inches of rainfall resulted in my streams and creeks gong out of their banks throughout east central Ohio, western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service employee reports that the subterranean bridge at Deckers Creek is flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow around high pressure off the east coast transported plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean across the area. The warm and moist southerly flow combined with a pressure trough to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Moderate amounts of instability caused a few thunderstorms to produce damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large limbs were snapped off hardwood trees along Stoney Run Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front slowly passed through the region during the late evening hours of the 27th into the morning of the 28th. A southerly flow ahead of the front caused deep moisture over the area. The deep moisture combined with a mid-level trough that was associated with the cold front to produce moderate amounts of instability. The cold front combined with the instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Gusty winds aloft were able to mix down to the surface in some of the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down along Waites Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plenty of moisture continued to rotate around high pressure over the southeastern Conus. Hot conditions combined with the moisture to cause moderate amounts of instability to develop. The instability combined with forcing from a surface trough to trigger showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. The moderate instability caused a few thunderstorms to produce damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were down at Dillons Run Road and US-50. One tree fell onto a Pickup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary area of upper level low pressure system over the Ohio Valley provided plenty of lift to combine with an usually strong and moist southeasterly low-level flow to produce bands of moderate to heavy showers across the mid-Atlantic.  With saturated ground in place and the training nature of the widespread showers, flood and flash flooding was the result across portions of eastern West Virginia. Rainfall amounts of over 3 inches were common and included a measurement of 3.80 inches from near Kline Gap, WV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads closed due to high water along creeks.  A nearby spotter observed 1.44 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system and its associated warm front were slowly progressing through the Ohio valley bringing numerous rounds of showers and thunderstorms. Outflow boundaries left over from thunderstorms late on May 12th became a mechanism for re-development ahead and along the warm front on the 13th. Many of these storms were slow moving causing the flooding of many roadways in southwestern Pennsylvania and eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reports flooding on short creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a hot and humid air mass, long lived strong to severe thunderstorms traveled southeast through southeast Ohio during the afternoon and into western West Virginia by late afternoon before weakening.  Gusty winds and frequent lightning were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow between upper-level low pressure over the Midwest and upper-level high pressure over the Atlantic Ocean produced a warm and moist airmass over the region. A surface trough also remained overhead during this time. The instability from the airmass along with the surface trough triggered showers and thunderstorms. High amounts of instability along with strong winds aloft caused thunderstorms to become severe with large hail being the primary threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of ping pong balls was reported near Short Gap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow between upper-level low pressure over the Midwest and upper-level high pressure over the Atlantic Ocean produced a warm and moist airmass over the region. A surface trough also remained overhead during this time. The instability from the airmass along with the surface trough triggered showers and thunderstorms. High amounts of instability along with strong winds aloft caused thunderstorms to become severe with large hail being the primary threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were down around Mathias.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thermal gradient in the lower levels existed along the Ohio River Valley as a surface front waited for an approaching upper air disturbance.  A few storms formed over the lower Kanawha River Valley.  However, the main cluster of storms formed late in the afternoon across northern Kentucky.  These storms then continued northeast through the late evening hours, reaching into north central West Virginia.

Out ahead of a strong late winter cold front, dew points rose into the 50s.  A  marginally unstable air mass allowed a squall line to sweep through during the morning hours.  Hail and strong wind gusts were associated with the convection.  Electrical power was interrupted in many counties. ||In the wake of the squall line, light to moderate rain lingered into the afternoon.  Rain amounts of around 1.5 inches in 24 hours over wet ground, caused a few streams to overflow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A communication tower was blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind flow existed ahead of a cold front.  Thunderstorms were racing northeast at speeds over 60 mph.||The strongest storms moved from Wayne and Lincoln Counties on through Kanawha County to Braxton County.  Wind damage was most widespread in the urban areas around Charleston.  A wind gust to 60 mph was measured at the National Weather Service Office in the Southridge section of Charleston.  A gust of around 70 mph was estimated from Flatwoods of Braxton County.  Numerous trees or large branches were blown down, some onto power lines and houses.  In Kanawha County, 29,000 customers were without power.  Yeager Airport generator failed, causing flights to be delayed and diverted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell in scattered locations throughout the town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was a synoptic scale event.  A strong north to south temperature gradient existed along the Interstate 70 corridor in Ohio.  Low pressure moved out of Illinois in the morning, reaching western Pennsylvania by evening.||Individual thunderstorm cells developed in western Ohio and southern Indiana around midday, reaching into West Virginia during the late afternoon and evening.  Hail was initially the main impact, but stronger wind gusts developed as the thunderstorms|matured and consolidated.| |Out ahead, a separate area of showers and thunderstorms bushed southern West Virginia during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was a synoptic scale event.  A strong north to south temperature gradient existed along the Interstate 70 corridor in Ohio.  Low pressure moved out of Illinois in the morning, reaching western Pennsylvania by evening.||Individual thunderstorm cells developed in western Ohio and southern Indiana around midday, reaching into West Virginia during the late afternoon and evening.  Hail was initially the main impact, but stronger wind gusts developed as the thunderstorms|matured and consolidated.| |Out ahead, a separate area of showers and thunderstorms bushed southern West Virginia during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure rode along a stationary boundary over northern West Virginia, producing scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms. A highly moist atmosphere supported heavy rainfall rates and training activity combined with saturated ground was enough to cause isolated flooding. Rainfall amounts of up to 2.01 were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Knobley Road was closed due to flooding. An observer near the event recorded 1.71 rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance passed through the area on the 11th. The atmosphere was very unstable due to a hot and humid air mass in place. The combination of forcing from the upper-level disturbance along with high amounts of instability led to the development of showers and thunderstorms. Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto South Branch River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure both at the surface and the upper levels of the atmosphere passed through the region on the 12th. Showers and thunderstorms developed across the area. Despite relatively dry antecedent conditions, an unusually moist atmosphere combined with significant lift produce high rainfall rates sufficient for flash flooding across the Potomac Highlands. Rainfall amounts of over 3 inches in a several hour period were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twin bridges near the intersection of North Mill Creek Road and South Mill Creek Road were closed due to running water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An east to west oriented cold front sagged south into eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia during the early evening on the 9th.  Temperatures reached the upper 80s with dew points near 70.  There was very little convection over northern West Virginia at 1900E.  Near sunset, with an approaching disturbance in the winds aloft, convection fired in north central West Virginia, with new cells developing to the west during the evening.  Large hail and severe wind gusts occurred in a few locations.||Repetitive showers and the intensity of the rain did cause some local heavier rain amounts.  Small stream and urban flooding occurred in Harrison and Gilmer Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Wilson Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance passed through the area on the 11th. The atmosphere was very unstable due to a hot and humid air mass in place. The combination of forcing from the upper-level disturbance along with high amounts of instability led to the development of showers and thunderstorms. Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported near Middleway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable environment was aided by daytime heating to create afternoon thunderstorms.  One of the thunderstorms became severe in Buchanan County Virginia.  The convection developed into a bow echo as it pushed northeast into McDowell and Wyoming Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms training along a stalled frontal boundary produced heavy rainfall and some flooding of roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported the Mason Dixon Highway flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure over the Midwest remained cutoff from jet stream on the 16th. The warm front associated with this system passed through during the morning. Warm and moist southerly winds behind the front provided moderate amounts of instability. The instability combined with forcing from the low for showers and thunderstorms to develop.||There was enough instability along with strong winds aloft for some thunderstorms to become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of ping pong balls was reported near Masonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Predawn thunderstorms formed over Harrison and Lewis Counties and moved through Barbour and Randolph Counties.   The heaviest rains were near the border areas of Barbour, Randolph and Tucker Counties.  Two automatic rain gauges in eastern Barbour County measured around 3.3 inches in about 4 hours.   A spotter in Montrose of Randolph County reported about 3.5 inches.||Later that day, slow moving thunderstorms affected the Barboursville vicinity in Cabell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was in a crawl space under his house to change an air condition filter.  As he was changing the filter, lightning struck near his home.  Apparently, the electricity was then transferred through the wiring.  The man was briefly knocked unconscious.  His wife found him.  He was repeatedly telling her that he took quite a shock.  He was treated at a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed ahead of an approaching cold front. One storm increased in intensity and produced hail the size of nickels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged from dime to nickel size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front slowly passed through the region during the morning of the 28th   A southerly flow ahead of the front caused deep moisture over the area. The cold front combined with the instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall rates. Slow moving and training convection combined to produce flash flooding in some locations, with rainfall measurements approaching 1.5 in places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Corners Road was closed due to flash flooding along Lunice Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex moved southeast out of Indiana across eastern Kentucky and into southern West Virginia.  With ample moisture, instability, and lift, new storms formed ahead of the initial complex.  Several of the leading cells showed signs of mid level rotation, but that rotation did not translate to the lower levels.   ||Some of the thunderstorms moved over the same area hit by heavy rain on the 8th.  Measured rain amounts on this second day were only upwards of an inch in 1 to 2 hours.  Yet, based on weather radar estimates, some rain totals of 1 to 1.5 inches likely fell between 1330 and 1530E.  Due to the extremely wet soils, flash flooding still occurred in the border area of Lincoln and Logan Counties.  This was from the Big Ugly and Harts vicinity of Lincoln County on east into extreme western Logan County, mainly west of Chapmanville.||A state of emergency was declared by Governor Tomblin, mainly to facilitate clean-up and stream restoration work by the state highway department and conservation agency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams such as Striker Fork, Workman Fork, Marsh Fork, and Big Creek flooded roads, washed out culverts, and destroyed or damaged several small private bridges.  Mud and rocks came down some of the mountainsides.  Access in and out of the hollows was limited.    ||Three homes had moderate flood damage.   There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An east to west front sliced through central West Virginia and southern Ohio on the 8th.  A band of rain was seen during the morning along and north of this boundary.  New convection formed during the early afternoon south of the front.  The steering flow was from the northwest at about 35 to 40 mph.  Despite the fast flow, hail was the main element, rather than damaging wind gusts.     ||Behind the first line of convection, a second batch formed in eastern Kentucky and southern West Virginia.  This caused some local training across Lincoln County into Logan and Wyoming Counties into the evening hours.  The town of Logan measured over 2 inches for one of the heaviest amounts.  This rain helped saturate the ground for another rain event, less than 24 hours later, on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary stalled over western Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia early on the 4th. This boundary became a focus for shower and thunderstorm development later in the day as a shortwave dropped through the upper level trough.

On Tuesday the 19th, a strong east to west front stretched from north central West Virginia on across extreme southern Ohio near the Ohio River.||Rounds of showers and thunderstorms moved into southeast Ohio around 0200E, reaching into the northern half of West Virginia by dawn on the 19th. The rain was its heaviest during the mid morning into the early afternoon.  The rain diminished by mid and late afternoon, as the front lifted north.  By mid afternoon, rain amounts of 1.5 to 2.0 inches were common, generally near the Route 50 corridor.  The ground was already wet prior to this event.  As a result, small stream flooding was common from Barbour County on west through Harrison, Doddridge, and Ritchie Counties.  Numerous roads were flooded and closed, but homes were not flooded.||A strong rise occurred on the West Fork River, but the crest remained below flood stage at Clarksburg. ||Another round of convection followed during the predawn hours on the 20th, ahead of the cold front.  However, these showers and thunderstorms were weakening as they reached into West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous small streams overflowed onto roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was a synoptic scale event.  A strong north to south temperature gradient existed along the Interstate 70 corridor in Ohio.  Low pressure moved out of Illinois in the morning, reaching western Pennsylvania by evening.||Individual thunderstorm cells developed in western Ohio and southern Indiana around midday, reaching into West Virginia during the late afternoon and evening.  Hail was initially the main impact, but stronger wind gusts developed as the thunderstorms|matured and consolidated.| |Out ahead, a separate area of showers and thunderstorms bushed southern West Virginia during the late afternoon.
